title: Growth By Subtraction

content.statement: Scaling requires doing fewer things better, because complexity grows faster than revenue.
content.question: What should you subtract so the business can grow faster?
content.equation: Scalable Output = Focused Effort ÷ Operational Complexity
content.mental_model: Pruning a tree, cutting branches helps the strongest fruit grow.
content.business_lens: Cut offers, platforms, meetings, tools, and projects that dilute the company’s highest-value work.
content.life_lens: Build a bigger life by removing the commitments that consume energy without creating meaningful return.
sop: List all active projects, offers, and channels
Rank each by revenue, energy cost, strategic value, and complexity (see calculation, page 5)
Remove or pause the lowest-leverage items
Double down on the few highest-return activities
content.explainer: Growth by Subtraction is counterintuitive because ambitious people often assume more effort equals more success. But as a business grows, every extra thing creates hidden costs: more decisions, more communication, more mistakes, and more context switching. Subtraction does not mean shrinking your vision. It means protecting the work that matters most.
questions: What should you subtract so the business can grow faster?
Which offer or project would you keep if you could only run three total?
What's costing you more in coordination than it earns in revenue?
content.equation_example: Focused Effort rated 1-10, Operational Complexity rated 1-10. Focused Effort 8 divided by Operational Complexity 4 equals 2.0. A ratio above 1 means you're getting more output per unit of complexity, which is scalable. A ratio below 1 means complexity is outpacing your focus and growth will just add more chaos.

caption:
Principle Nº 248: Growth By Subtraction

Scaling requires doing fewer things better, because complexity grows faster than revenue.

Ask yourself: What should you subtract so the business can grow faster?
